content.assessment: No comparable international case of fiscal-conditionality-based detracking or admissions-equity reform is present in the supplied evidence. The comparative record addresses tracking-age and tracking-structure reforms almost exclusively; a funding-lever mechanism targeting private-preparation-dependent admission has no cross-national precedent in this evidence set, making assessment through this lens highly limited.
opportunities: Could, if implemented, generate novel Hungary-specific evidence potentially informative for other systems, given the absence of prior international examples in the record
strengths: Does not directly contradict any comparative finding; operates on a different policy lever (fiscal) than the structural/admissions mechanisms evaluated internationally, so no negative transfer evidence applies
threats: Absence of any comparable precedent increases uncertainty about whether fiscal levers can counteract the compositional/status-sorting dynamics identified in Hungary-specific panel evidence as central to the inequality mechanism
weaknesses: No comparator country's mechanism in the evidence set resembles fiscal conditionality tied to equity-outcome measurement, so transferability and outcome relevance cannot be assessed from this lens

Cross-national evidence on tracking effects centers on structural and compositional sorting mechanisms (value-added and composition effects in Hungary-specific tracks), not on funding-based incentives, leaving a mechanism gap unaddressed by comparative findings
